question_text: A manager at a textile factory spends most of the day on the shop floor ensuring workers operate machines correctly and follow safety protocols. This is an example of:
explanation: Supervision involves overseeing the work of subordinates at the workplace. The manager is directly monitoring and guiding workers — a classic supervisory function within directing.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Supervision
Motivation
Communication
Controlling

elimination_hints.hint: Motivation involves inspiring employees — the manager here is monitoring, not inspiring.
Communication is exchanging information — the manager is physically overseeing work.
Controlling measures performance against standards — this is day-to-day overseeing at the shop floor.

question_text: An employee at a call centre consistently receives praise from the manager and is given 'Employee of the Month' award. According to Maslow, which need is being satisfied?
explanation: Praise and recognition satisfy esteem needs (fourth level). Esteem needs include self-respect, recognition, status, and attention from others. Being publicly recognised as 'Employee of the Month' directly addresses the need for esteem.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Physiological needs
Safety needs
Esteem needs
Self-actualisation needs

elimination_hints.hint: Physiological needs are basic survival needs — praise and awards don't address hunger or shelter.
Safety needs involve security and stability — the award is about recognition, not safety.
Self-actualisation is about reaching full potential — an award is external recognition (esteem), not self-fulfilment.

question_text: A company offers its employees stock options, profit-sharing plans, and performance bonuses. These are examples of:
explanation: Stock options, profit-sharing, and bonuses are all financial incentives as they involve direct or indirect monetary benefits to employees. They motivate employees through economic rewards linked to performance.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Non-financial incentives
Financial incentives
Organisational barriers
Elements of communication

elimination_hints.hint: Stock options and bonuses have clear monetary value — they are financial, not non-financial.
These are motivation tools, not communication barriers.
Communication elements are sender, message, channel — not compensation tools.

question_text: In laissez-faire (free-rein) leadership, the leader:
explanation: In laissez-faire leadership, the leader gives complete freedom to subordinates. The leader provides minimal guidance, avoids making decisions, and lets the group function on its own. It works best with highly skilled and self-motivated teams.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Makes all decisions and expects strict obedience
Consults team members before making every decision
Closely supervises every task performed by subordinates
Gives complete freedom to subordinates and avoids decision-making

elimination_hints.hint: Making all decisions and expecting obedience is autocratic leadership.
Consulting before decisions is democratic leadership.
Close supervision is the opposite of laissez-faire, which means 'leave it alone'.

question_text: Which type of communication flows from subordinate to superior?
explanation: Upward communication flows from subordinates to superiors — for example, reports, suggestions, complaints, and grievances sent by employees to their managers. It provides feedback on how well the organisation is functioning.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Downward communication
Upward communication
Horizontal communication
Diagonal communication

elimination_hints.hint: Downward communication flows from superior to subordinate — the opposite direction.
Horizontal communication is between peers at the same level, not subordinate to superior.
Diagonal communication crosses both levels and departments — it's not specifically subordinate to superior.

question_text: A company provides its employees with a well-furnished recreation room, flexible working hours, and a suggestion box. These measures primarily aim to satisfy:
explanation: Recreation rooms address social needs (relaxation, belonging), flexible hours provide autonomy (esteem), and suggestion boxes give employees a voice (esteem/self-actualisation). Together, these are non-financial incentives addressing higher-order needs.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Only physiological needs through monetary means
Only safety needs through job security
Social and esteem needs through non-financial incentives
Only self-actualisation needs

elimination_hints.hint: None of these measures involve money — they are non-financial and address higher needs.
These are not about job security — they're about creating a positive work environment.
While partially addressing self-actualisation, these primarily satisfy social and esteem needs.

question_text: A key assumption of Maslow's Need Hierarchy Theory is:
explanation: Maslow assumes that once a need is substantially satisfied, it ceases to be a motivator, and the next higher level of need becomes active. People are motivated by unsatisfied needs in the hierarchy.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: A satisfied need no longer motivates; the next higher need becomes the motivator
All five needs are active at the same time with equal intensity
Only financial incentives can satisfy any need in the hierarchy
Self-actualisation is the first need to be satisfied

elimination_hints.hint: Maslow says needs follow a hierarchy — lower needs must be substantially satisfied before higher ones activate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all needs are equal and simultaneous

elimination_hints.hint: Higher-order needs (social, esteem, self-actualisation) are satisfied by non-financial means.
Self-actualisation is the last (highest) need — it activates only after all lower needs are met.

question_text: Directing is a pervasive function because:
explanation: Directing is pervasive because every manager at every level — from top to supervisory — must direct their subordinates. It takes place wherever there is a superior-subordinate relationship in the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: It is performed only by the CEO of the company
It takes place at every level of management wherever there is a superior-subordinate relationship
It is needed only during the initial years of a company
It applies only to manufacturing industries

elimination_hints.hint: Directing is not limited to the CEO — every manager directs their team.
Directing is a continuous function — it is needed throughout the life of an organisation.
Directing is universal — it applies to all types of organisations, not just manufacturing.

question_text: An organisational barrier to effective communication is:
explanation: Organisational barriers include rigid rules and policies, status differences, complexity of organisational structure, and lack of proper communication channels. Too many hierarchical levels distort messages as they pass through multiple people.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Use of different languages by sender and receiver
Emotional state of the receiver
Loud machinery on the factory floor
Too many levels in the organisational hierarchy

elimination_hints.hint: Different languages is a semantic barrier, not organisational.
Emotional state is a psychological/personal barrier.
Machinery noise is a physical/mechanical barrier.

question_text: Which financial incentive involves employees receiving a share of profits beyond their normal wages?
explanation: Profit sharing is a financial incentive where employees receive a share of the organisation's profits in addition to their regular wages. It creates a sense of partnership between employees and the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Perquisites (perks)
Retirement benefits
Profit sharing
Job enrichment

elimination_hints.hint: Perks like company car, housing are additional benefits — not a direct share of profits.
Retirement benefits (pension, gratuity) are post-retirement financial security, not current profit-sharing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing retirement benefits with profit sharing

elimination_hints.hint: Job enrichment is a non-financial incentive — it makes work more meaningful, not more profitable for the employee.

question_text: A CEO sends an email to all department heads about a new company policy. This is an example of:
explanation: When a superior (CEO) sends information to subordinates (department heads), it is downward communication. It flows from top to bottom through the official chain of command.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Downward communication
Upward communication
Horizontal communication
Grapevine communication

elimination_hints.hint: Upward flows from subordinate to superior — here the CEO (superior) is sending to department heads (subordinates).
Horizontal is between peers at the same level — CEO to department heads is top-down.
Grapevine is informal — an official email about company policy is formal communication.

question_text: Co-partnership or stock option as a financial incentive means:
explanation: Co-partnership/stock option allows employees to buy shares of the company at a discounted price or receive shares as part of compensation. This gives employees a sense of ownership and motivates them to work for the company's success.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Employees receive a fixed bonus regardless of company performance
Employees are offered shares of the company, making them part-owners
Employees are given free housing by the company
Employees rotate through different departments for training

elimination_hints.hint: Fixed bonus regardless of performance is a regular bonus — not the same as co-partnership/stock option.
Free housing is a perquisite (perk), not co-partnership.
Job rotation is a training method, not a financial incentive.

question_text: Directing flows from:
explanation: Directing flows from top to bottom. It starts at the top management level and flows downward through middle management to supervisory level. Every manager directs their immediate subordinates.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Top to bottom in the management hierarchy
Bottom to top as employees direct their managers
Horizontally between departments at the same level
Only within the HR department

elimination_hints.hint: Subordinates don't direct managers — directing flows from superior to subordinate.
Directing is vertical (top to bottom) — horizontal coordination is a different aspect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing direction flow with lateral coordination

elimination_hints.hint: Directing is pervasive — it happens in every department, not just HR.

question_text: An employee who has completed a challenging project feels a deep sense of achievement and fulfilment. According to Maslow, this satisfies:
explanation: A deep sense of achievement and fulfilment from completing a challenging project reflects self-actualisation — the highest need in Maslow's hierarchy. It is about realising one's potential and feeling internally fulfilled.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Physiological needs
Safety needs
Esteem needs
Self-actualisation needs

elimination_hints.hint: This is about internal fulfilment, not basic survival needs.
Completing a project gives fulfilment, not security — this is higher than safety needs.
Esteem is about recognition from others — this is about internal sense of achievement and reaching potential.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing internal fulfilment with external recognition

question_text: The term 'feedback' in communication refers to:
explanation: Feedback is the response or reaction of the receiver to the sender's message. It completes the communication cycle and helps the sender know whether the message has been understood correctly.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: The channel through which the message is sent
The encoding process used by the sender
The receiver's response to the sender's message
The noise that distorts the message

elimination_hints.hint: The channel (email, phone, face-to-face) is the medium, not the response.
Encoding is converting the message into a suitable form — done by the sender, not the receiver.
Noise is interference that distorts communication — feedback is the intended response.

question_text: Career advancement opportunities and employee participation in decision-making are examples of:
explanation: Career advancement and participation in decisions are non-financial incentives. They satisfy higher-order needs (esteem, self-actualisation) without involving direct monetary payment. They motivate through psychological satisfaction.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Financial incentives
Semantic barriers
Elements of the communication process
Non-financial incentives

elimination_hints.hint: Career opportunities and participation don't involve direct monetary payment — they are non-financial.
Semantic barriers are communication problems, not motivational tools.
Communication elements are sender, message, channel — not career opportunities.

question_text: A personal barrier to communication occurs when:
explanation: Personal barriers arise from the attitudes, emotions, status, and mindset of the sender or receiver. Lack of trust, fear of authority, unwillingness to communicate, or poor listening habits are personal barriers.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: The message is too complex with technical terminology
The receiver distrusts the sender and ignores the message
The communication channel breaks down due to technical failure
The organisation has too many hierarchical levels

elimination_hints.hint: Complex terminology is a semantic barrier — about language, not personal attitudes.
Technical failure of the channel is a physical/mechanical barrier.
Too many hierarchy levels is an organisational barrier.

question_text: A team leader at Infosys discusses project goals with team members, takes their suggestions, and then makes the final decision. This is an example of:
explanation: The leader discusses goals, takes suggestions (participative), but makes the final decision. This is democratic leadership — subordinates participate in decision-making, though the leader retains the final authority.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Autocratic leadership
Democratic leadership
Laissez-faire leadership
Bureaucratic leadership

elimination_hints.hint: Autocratic leaders don't discuss or take suggestions — they decide alone.
Laissez-faire leaders would let the team decide entirely — here the leader makes the final decision.
Bureaucratic leadership follows rigid rules — this scenario shows flexibility and consultation.

question_text: Which of the following best describes the role of a supervisor?
explanation: A supervisor is the link between workers and management. They guide day-to-day activities, maintain discipline, ensure quality, and relay information both upward and downward. They are the first-line managers in the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Acts as a link between workers and management, guiding day-to-day work
Sets the overall strategic vision for the company
Handles all financial planning and budgeting
Designs the organisational structure and reporting relationships

elimination_hints.hint: Strategic vision is set by top management (CEO, Board), not supervisors.
Financial planning is done by finance managers, not supervisors.
Designing organisational structure is an organising function at top management level.

question_text: Directing is a continuous function because:
explanation: Directing is continuous because managers must constantly guide, motivate, supervise, and communicate with employees throughout the life of the organisation. It doesn't stop after initial instructions — it happens every day at every level.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: It is done only once when the organisation is established
It is needed only during a crisis situation
Managers must constantly guide, motivate, and communicate with employees
It is an annual activity performed during performance reviews

elimination_hints.hint: Directing is ongoing — not a one-time activity at establishment.
Directing is needed during normal operations too, not just during crises.
Directing happens daily, not just annually — performance reviews are part of staffing.
